stories.title: ESD pitches $5 billion plan to complete Pacific Park
stories.excerpt: Pacific Park, the long delayed plan to cover railyards in a busy patch of Brooklyn, may finally move forward after Empire State Development and the project’s community development corporation board pitched a $5 billion buildout that would build more than 5,600 residential units, more than 1,200 of which would be income-restricted. The beleaguered project, first dubbed Atlantic Yards and still unfinished after more than two decades, originally called for $2.5 billion when former lead developer Bruce Ratner first announced it in 2003 alongside big names including late starchitect Frank Gehry and Brooklyn-born hip-hop star Jay-Z.  The Atlantic Yards Community Development […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Empire State Development pitches $5B to complete Brooklyn's Pacific Park with 5,600+ units
25% income-restricted units (moderate/low income); 30% family-sized; union labor required
Project stalled 20+ years; covers railyards Atlantic to Vanderbilt Avenue plus Flatbush sites
stories.summary: Empire State Development and the Atlantic Yards CDC plan a $5 billion completion of Pacific Park in Brooklyn, adding 5,600+ residential units across multiple phases, with roughly 1,200 income-restricted units (25% moderate, 75% low income) and 30% family-sized units. The megaproject also includes 1,000 condo units and covers railyards between Atlantic and Vanderbilt Avenues plus additional Flatbush Avenue sites. Originally announced in 2003 at $2.5B, the project has languished for two decades under multiple developers including defunct Forest City Ratner and current owner Greenland USA (Shanghai-based).
stories.impact: A 5,600-unit supply infusion into Brooklyn's tight rental market signals major affordability-focused density approved at city level, setting precedent for mixed-income multifamily entitlements in central Brooklyn. The 20-year timeline also highlights regulatory and financing friction; union labor mandate and 75% low-income units compress margins, requiring public subsidy or patient capital—watch for financing structures that shape development feasibility across NYC.

stories.title: Dezer targets broader market with North Miami residential development
stories.excerpt: The Dezer father-son team is moving forward with Dezerland Park West after the proposed 586-unit project in North Miami was unanimously approved by the city’s planning board.  Sunny Isles Beach-based Dezer Development, led by Michael Dezer and son Gil Dezer, is planning a two-building residential development for the 4.44-acre site at 1890 Northeast 146th Street, the South Florida Business Journal reported. The site is currently used as a truck parking lot.  A 14-story building with 269 apartments and 4,500 square feet of retail space, plus an eight-story parking garage, will sit on the northern parcel of the site. An […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Dezer Development approved for 586-unit North Miami project (269 + 317 units) on former truck lot
Market-rate focus; transit-overlay zoning allowed residential on industrial-zoned site; $24.5M in fees to city
Units 500–1,500 sq ft; 40–50 permanent jobs; $40.5M local spending estimated
stories.summary: Dezer Development won unanimous planning board approval for Dezerland Park West, a two-building, 586-unit market-rate multifamily project in North Miami on a 4.44-acre former industrial site zoned for truck parking. The development leverages transit-oriented overlay zoning to permit residential use and includes 14 and 8-story buildings with retail, parking, townhouses, and 20,000 sq ft of amenities. The developer commits $24.5M in impact/permit fees and will upgrade nearby roads; economic impact estimated at $40.5M local spending.
stories.impact: North Miami's transit-overlay zoning enabling industrial-to-residential conversion signals municipal appetite for density near transit, lowering entitlement risk for similar infill sites in underutilized corridors. Dezer's market-rate positioning (vs. affordable mandate) and approval speed indicate strong local demand; similar sites in South Florida's TOD-zoned areas may be ripe for acquisition before upzoning becomes priced in.

stories.title: CREFC’s Lisa Pendergast Sees Industry Moving into “Constructive Period” (VIDEO)
stories.excerpt: At the CREFC Annual Conference held earlier this month, CEO Lisa Pendergast summed up the current state of commercial real estate finance in an interview with Connect CRE. She characterized the industry as moving into a “constructive period,” despite the challenges of high interest rates. “We have a significant amount–billions of dollars of loans that are maturing, that have 4% coupons on their mortgage rate, and yet we’re now talking somewhere in the 6.5% – 7% [range] if you were to refinance today,” Pendergast told Connect CRE. She added that she was “not too optimistic” that we’ll be seeing significant rate cuts soon, but once the elevated inflation rate comes down, refinancing can proceed in a much more “accretive” way. In the video below, Pendergast also reflected on a CRE finance career that has spanned decades, and offered advice to industry members in the early stages of their own careers. The post CREFC’s Lisa Pendergast Sees Industry Moving into “Constructive Period” (VIDEO) appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: CREFC CEO: billions of maturing loans at 4% coupon face 6.5–7% refi rates; rate cuts delayed
Industry entering 'constructive period' as maturity wall compresses; refinancing accretive when inflation falls
High rates persist; loan repricing gap creates refinancing friction through 2025–2026
stories.summary: CREFC CEO Lisa Pendergast characterized the CRE finance market as moving into a 'constructive period' despite a significant maturity wall: billions in loans originated at ~4% coupons must refinance into 6.5–7% market rates. She cautioned against near-term rate cuts but noted refinancing will become more 'accretive' once inflation normalizes, allowing distressed debt to resolve without forced sales.
stories.impact: Refinancing friction persists; multifamily sponsors holding legacy floating-rate or low-coupon debt face material resets in 2025–2027 windows. Underwriting spreads and cap rate assumptions must account for 200–300 bps refi headwinds; assets with strong NOI and occupancy may refinance without loss, but leveraged or transitional properties will compress equity or require workout structures. Expect selective distress and portfolio sales from overleveraged holders.

stories.title: NYC developer in hot pursuit of Albany data center
stories.excerpt: Even as a data center moratorium sits on Gov. Kathy Hochul’s desk, awaiting a signature, a zoning change could push forward a development right in the state capital. EKG Group’s Michael-Henry Elghanian-Krayem is pushing to rezone into the “light industrial” classification at the Kenwood Commons site, the Times Union reported. The 76-acre site is zoned for a mixed-use campus today and needs numerous approvals to go forward, including at least two revisions to the city’s zoning regulations. The rezoning would allow for both a data center and a broader technology campus. The $570 million project consists of a 500,000-square-foot data […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: EKG Group seeks Albany zoning to light-industrial for $570M+ data center + tech campus + 300 apartments
Kenwood Commons site: 500k sq ft data center, research labs, offices, ground-floor retail; upside to $2B
Community opposition focuses on affordability; data center moratorium pending Gov. Hochul signature
stories.summary: EKG Group's Michael-Henry Elghanian-Krayem is pursuing a zoning change for Albany's 76-acre Kenwood Commons site from mixed-use to light-industrial to enable a $570M tech campus including a 500,000 sq ft data center, research labs, offices, 300 apartments, and retail, with potential upside to $2B. The project faces community pushback favoring affordable housing; Elghanian-Krayem argues the site economics don't work without state/federal subsidies. A state data center moratorium awaits signature, creating regulatory uncertainty.
stories.impact: Bundling 300 apartments into a data-center-anchored campus suggests the developer sees residential as amenity/retention tool rather than primary revenue driver—typical for tech campuses. If the moratorium passes, Albany loses this mixed-use opportunity; if it dies in committee, the project opens a blueprint for data-center-plus-housing on industrial sites across upstate NY. Watch whether the residential density is truly market-rate or subsidy-dependent; if the latter, it signals data centers cannot economically cross-fund housing without public aid in non-coastal metros.

stories.title: Private Equity Firm Signs Full-Floor Lease at Marx Realty’s 545 Madison
stories.excerpt: Niobrara Capital, a private equity firm focused on investing in technology and technology-enabled services businesses, has signed an 11,000-square-foot lease for the entire ninth floor at Marx Realty’s 545 Madison, located in Midtown Manhattan’s Plaza District. The property is also known as the Baccarat Building following Marx Realty’s office co-branding partnership with lifestyle house Baccarat. “The continued leasing momentum at 545 Madison speaks to the power of pairing this exceptional Plaza District address with a truly differentiated hospitality-infused experience,” said Craig Deitelzweig, president and CEO of Marx Realty. “The building’s warm, club-like sensibility, refined design and unmistakable Baccarat touches continue to resonate with premier firms looking for a workplace that feels elevated, elegant and distinctive.” A Newmark team led by CJ Heitner and John Cilmi represented Niobrara Capital. Marx Realty was represented by Tara Stacom, Peter Trivelas, Harry Blair, and Remy Leibersohn of Cushman & Wakefield. The post Private Equity Firm Signs Full-Floor Lease at Marx Realty’s 545 Madison appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Niobrara Capital (PE firm) leases full 9th floor (11k sq ft) at Marx Realty's 545 Madison, Midtown
Plaza District office co-branded with Baccarat luxury house; hospitality-infused workplace positioning
No metrics on lease rate, tenant demand breadth, or market occupancy—single-lease signal only
stories.summary: Niobrara Capital, a PE firm, signed an 11,000 sq ft full-floor lease at 545 Madison (Baccarat Building) in Midtown Manhattan's Plaza District. Marx Realty has differentiated the asset via luxury co-branding with Baccarat, targeting high-end tenants seeking 'club-like' office space. Newmark and Cushman & Wakefield brokered the deal.
stories.impact: Minimal relevance to multifamily development. This is a single office lease in Manhattan's Plaza District; no demand signal for rental housing, construction, or capital flows affecting apartment markets. The Baccarat branding signals office landlords competing on amenity/experience, not supply/demand dynamics affecting multifamily.

stories.title: LA Condo Property Sells to Its Original Developer
stories.excerpt: Marcus & Millichap closed the sale of 11925 Kling St., a 48-condominium property in the Valley Village neighborhood of Los Angeles. The condominiums sold for an average price of $331,250 each. Tony Azzi and Rabbie Banafsheha of the Azzi Group at Marcus & Millichap in Los Angeles represented the seller, MLT VII LLC. Azzi, Banafsheha, Arteen Zahiri and Ian Habbestad of Marcus & Millichap procured the buyer, a funding investment corporation.  “Valley Village is one of the most sought-after residential neighborhoods in the San Fernando Valley, where average household income has grown by 45 percent since 2000, and the median price of a single-family home is $755,000,” said Azzi. Added Banafsheha, “The property’s 48 individually parceled units give the buyer maximum flexibility for multiple exit strategies. The winning bidder happened to be the developer who built the property in 2012.” The post LA Condo Property Sells to Its Original Developer appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Original developer repurchases 48-unit Valley Village condo building; $331k average unit price
Property built 2012, sold back to developer via funding corp.; no debt/cap rate disclosed
Valley Village avg household income +45% since 2000; median single-family home $755k
stories.summary: A 48-unit condo building at 11925 Kling St. in Valley Village, Los Angeles, sold for ~$15.9M (~$331k/unit) back to its 2012 original developer via a funding investment corporation. Brokers (Marcus & Millichap) noted individually parceled units offer 'maximum flexibility' for buyer exit strategies and highlighted strong neighborhood fundamentals: +45% income growth since 2000 and $755k median single-family home price.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. A condo-for-sale building reselling to its original developer signals either stabilization of the asset or recycling for alternative use (conversion, land play). The income growth and SFH price appreciation in Valley Village suggest strong residential demand, but this deal does not indicate new supply, capital availability, or zoning changes affecting rental development in the San Fernando Valley.

stories.title: Crown Heights Office/Flex Property Garners Bridge Loan for Refi
stories.excerpt: Arrow Real Estate Advisors arranged a $22,000,000 bridge refinance for 1000 Dean St., a creative office and flex property located in Brooklyn’s Crown Heights/Prospect Heights submarket. The financing was arranged by Arrow’s Morris Betesh, Israel Mermelstein, Jack McPhail, Alex Ellman and Joseph Kean, and was provided by Genesis Credit LLC. The loan refinances a 129,389-square-foot asset that serves a diverse mix of creative, office, production, and flex tenants in one of Brooklyn’s most active commercial corridors. It required a financing partner capable of understanding the property’s transitionalnature and the nuances associated with its ongoing lease-up strategy. “This was a nuanced transaction given the transitional profile of the asset, but Genesis quickly understood the strength of the location, the quality of the collateral and the sponsor’s business plan,” said Betesh. “We appreciate Genesis’ partnership and ability to provide a flexible solution that supports the continued stabilization of the property.” Photo courtesy of Selldorf Architects. The post Crown Heights Office/Flex Property Garners Bridge Loan for Refi appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Arrow Real Estate arranges $22M bridge refi for 1000 Dean St., Crown Heights creative office/flex
129k sq ft transitional asset with diverse tenants (creative, production, flex); lease-up ongoing
Genesis Credit provided flexible financing for nuanced transitional profile and location strength
stories.summary: Arrow Real Estate arranged a $22M bridge refinance through Genesis Credit for 1000 Dean St., a 129,389 sq ft creative office and flex property in Brooklyn's Crown Heights/Prospect Heights corridor. The asset serves a mix of creative, office, production, and flex tenants and is undergoing ongoing lease-up, requiring a lender comfortable with transitional risk and business-plan flexibility.
stories.impact: Minimal direct relevance to multifamily development. This is office/flex financing in Brooklyn; no demand or supply signal for apartments. However, it does highlight that strong Brooklyn locations (Prospect Heights/Crown Heights) are attracting flexible capital even for transitional commercial—a halo for nearby multifamily submarkets with strong tenant demand and walkability.

stories.title: Citigroup Refis East Village Apartment Building With $45M CMBS Loan
stories.excerpt: Benchmark Real Estate Group has sealed $44.5 million of commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) debt to refinance a multifamily property in Manhattan’s East Village, Commercial Observer has learned. Citigroup provided the CMBS loan for the 61-unit, six-story apartment building at 194 East Second Street that Benchmark acquired from Skyline Developers in 2024 for $43 million. JLL negotiated the refi with a debt advisory team led by Michael Zaremski, John Flynn and Clayton Ross. “Demand for high-quality multifamily assets in Manhattan continues to be driven by exceptionally strong operating fundamentals, limited new supply and sustained renter demand,” Zaremski said in a statement. “194 East Second Street represents a rare combination of institutional-quality amenities.” Located at the corner of East Second Street and Avenue B, the six-story property underwent renovations following Benchmark’s acquisition two years ago, including upgrades to individual units, common areas and amenity spaces, according to JLL. Community amenities include a fitness center, a sauna, a yoga studio, a Pilates room and a courtyard. The 194 East Second property also features roughly 15,450 square feet of retail space anchored by Duane Reade. “This transaction is a compelling example of how the debt capital markets are evolving to meet sponsor demand for well-leased, institutional-quality assets,” Ross said in a statement. “Citi’s multifamily-only CMBS portfolio creates a more efficient execution path for sponsors like Benchmark who own high-quality, stabilized properties.” Citigroup and Benchmark Real Estate Group did not immediately return requests for comment. Andrew Coen can be reached at acoen@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Benchmark Real Estate sealed $44.5M CMBS refi for 61-unit East Village building; 2024 acquisition at $43M
Manhattan multifamily driven by strong fundamentals, limited supply, sustained renter demand
Post-acquisition renovations to units, amenities; institutional-quality property positioning
stories.summary: Benchmark Real Estate closed a $44.5M CMBS refinance (via Citigroup) for a 61-unit, six-story apartment building at 194 East Second Street in Manhattan's East Village. Benchmark acquired the property from Skyline Developers in 2024 for $43M and subsequently renovated units and common areas. JLL's advisory team noted exceptional Manhattan multifamily fundamentals: strong operations, limited new supply, and sustained renter demand supporting institutional-quality asset valuations.
stories.impact: CMBS market appetite for Manhattan multifamily remains robust despite recent underwriting tightness; a quick refi at near-acquisition price signals no value destruction and lender confidence in East Village submarket fundamentals. This validates aggressive 2024 acquisition pricing; expect similar trophy multifamily assets in Manhattan to clear financing and support further institutional buyer entry. Limited-supply thesis underpins continued rent growth in Prime Manhattan neighborhoods, supporting development risk mitigation on new supply pipelines.

stories.title: Mogharebi Group Forges Partnership with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital
stories.excerpt: The Mogharebi Group (TMG) has formed an exclusive partnership with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital LLC, a subsidiary of Truist Bank. The partnership provides TMG clients with direct access to a lending partner. “This partnership is unique in that it enhances our services to our clients by providing a dedicated, highly capable lending partner into the investment process,” said EVP Otto Ozen of Costa Mesa-based TMG. “By combining TMG’s market expertise with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital’s national platform, agency lending capabilities, and expanding geographic reach, we are able to deliver improved access to capital and a more efficient path to closing, whether a sale or refinance.” Grandbridge Real Estate Capital sources permanent commercial and multifamily real estate loans; services loan portfolios; and provides asset and portfolio management through its broad investor base that includes insurance companies, CMBS investors, pension fund advisors, commercial banks and capital markets investors. The post Mogharebi Group Forges Partnership with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital appeared first on Connect CRE.
bullets: Mogharebi Group partners with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital (Truist Bank subsidiary) for exclusive lending access
Grandbridge sources permanent commercial/multifamily loans; insurance, CMBS, pension, bank investor base
Partnership improves capital access and closing efficiency for TMG clients across geographies
stories.summary: The Mogharebi Group (TMG), a Costa Mesa-based real estate firm, formed an exclusive partnership with Grandbridge Real Estate Capital, a Truist Bank subsidiary. Grandbridge provides permanent multifamily and commercial real estate financing sourced from insurance companies, CMBS investors, pension funds, and commercial banks, enabling TMG to offer clients direct lending access and faster closing paths for acquisitions and refinances.
stories.impact: Partnership signals continued capital availability and streamlined debt sourcing for multifamily investors in TMG's western markets (California, Southwest). Truist's expansion of multifamily lending via Grandbridge indicates banking sector confidence in rental asset stability; improved capital access may accelerate acquisition pace and lower financing friction, particularly for value-add and stabilized assets in Sunbelt/Western markets where TMG operates.

stories.title: Bonaventure Raises $54M for Two Virginia Multifamily Properties
stories.excerpt: A multifamily investment firm has closed two financing vehicles that combine for $54.2 million for two Class A apartment developments in Virginia. Bonaventure announced Monday that 86 investors committed equity to two Delaware Statutory Trust offerings, which are investment structures that allow multiple investors to own fractional interests through a trust rather than owning a property directly. Bonaventure acquired the first property, Messenger Place in Downtown Manassas, Va., for $25.5 million in 2021, two years after it was completed. The 94-unit multifamily community is 95.7 occupied, and it raised $34.1 million from 41 investors. Bonaventure developed the second property, Promenade Pointe, about four miles north of Downtown Norfolk, in 2013. The 183-unit waterfront community is 94 percent occupied, and it raised $20.1 million in equity from 45 investors. “Different investors need different paths, and the right structure depends on where someone is in their life and their portfolio,” Casey MacMaster, senior vice president at Bonaventure, said in a statement. “Some are completing a 1031 exchange; others are seeking diversification or a step toward long-term ownership.” Alexandria, Va.-based Bonaventure has more than $2.9 billion in assets under management. Earlier this year, CEO and founder Dwight Dunton sat down with Commercial Observer to discuss his firm’s business strategies and what separates the company from other multifamily owners and operators. Gregory Cornfield can be reached at gcornfield@commercialobserver.com.
bullets: Bonaventure closes $54.2M equity via DST offerings for two Virginia multifamily properties
Messenger Place (Manassas): 94 units, $34.1M raised, 95.7% occupied; Promenade Pointe (Norfolk): 183 units, $20.1M raised
86 investors; DST structure allows fractional ownership; established asset recycling and fractional investment trend
stories.summary: Bonaventure announced $54.2M in equity commitments across two Delaware Statutory Trust offerings for two Virginia multifamily properties: Messenger Place (94 units, Downtown Manassas, acquired 2021 for $25.5M, 95.7% occupied, $34.1M equity raised from 41 investors) and Promenade Pointe (183 units, waterfront Norfolk, developed 2013, 94% occupied, $20.1M equity raised from 45 investors). A total of 86 investors participated in the DST structures.
stories.impact: DST packaging of stabilized Virginia multifamily assets for fractional investor access signals strong demand for stable, regional multifamily cash flows; Bonaventure's ability to syndicate established assets at scale (277 total units) suggests investor appetite for lower-volatility multifamily in Tier-2/3 metros. High occupancy and fractional-capital solutions indicate investor positioning for steady income over appreciation—typical of maturing assets and crowded capital markets. Watch for continued DST/1031 syndication of stabilized multifamily as institutional equity tightens and sponsors recycle capital.

stories.title: HSF Kramer’s Seth Niedermayer On Architecting Private Equity’s Real Estate Rise
stories.excerpt: HSF Kramer’s Seth Niedermayer has a front row seat to the rewiring of U.S. real estate investment. The law partner currently has 17 deals on his plate, the majority of which have private equity in the driver’s seat. Among those transactions are partner-side joint ventures, representations for lenders and borrowers on construction loans, and a sale-leaseback transaction. Niedermayer’s workload fluctuates frequently, but private capital is dominating the stack, literally and figuratively. Private equity’s growing dominance is yielding highly structured deals, a lot more leverage and a booming debt fund business. The increasing complexity of real estate investments, paired with high interest rates and market uncertainty, has the effect of slowing negotiations. As a consequence, Niedermayer’s to-do list is full and then some. He’s not complaining, though. Niedermayer says he thrives on variety, making him well suited to the dynamism of HSF Kramer’s real estate group. The Bethesda, Md., native comes by his skill set honestly. His father still practices as a litigator, and his mother worked in governmental law. To top it all off, he has a New York City real estate broker for a brother. When Niedermayer earned his juris doctor at Columbia Law School, he chose an alternative path from his parents, in the “accretive and constructive” work of navigating deals and advising transactions. So much so that a colleague of his brother once mistook him for a broker. “I like introducing people to each other and saying, ‘You guys should talk, there might be something here,’” Niedermayer, 42, said. “It just feels like you’re building something, as opposed to litigation, when you’re fighting over something.” From novice to expert Niedermayer departed international law firm Simpson Thacher & Bartlett in 2015 to join HSF Kramer, then known as Kramer Levin Naftalis & Frankel. He arrived as an associate with hardly any real estate experience, but Dan Berman, managing part
bullets: HSF Kramer partner Seth Niedermayer manages 17 deals, majority PE-driven joint ventures and debt structures
PE dominance yielding complex leverage, structured deals, and booming debt fund financing
High rates and market uncertainty slowing deal negotiations; complex capital stacks becoming standard
stories.summary: HSF Kramer law partner Seth Niedermayer reports a heavy workload dominated by private equity-led real estate deals, including partner-side JVs, lender/borrower construction financing, and sale-leaseback structures. PE's growing prominence is driving increased leverage, complex capital stacks, and a boom in debt-fund financing. High interest rates and market uncertainty are lengthening deal timelines, but the complexity and variety are sustaining robust legal and advisory demand.
stories.impact: PE's dominance in deal flow underscores a shift toward leveraged structures and hybrid capital sources (debt funds, mezz, preferred equity) in real estate financing. For multifamily developers, this means deal partners will increasingly be PE sponsors or debt-fund-backed buyers, requiring sophisticated underwriting and exit coordination. The complexity and lengthened negotiation timelines suggest extended development timelines and higher advisory costs; sponsors should budget for longer closing periods and multi-tranche financing.

stories.title: René Morkos of ALICE Technologies: 5 Questions
stories.excerpt: René Morkos, founder and CEO at ALICE Technologies, which is billed as the world’s first artificial intelligence-powered construction optimization platform, is no stranger to AI, contech or proptech. However, he is catching up to the benefits of his company’s new alliance with McKinsey & Company, which was announced in April. A Stanford-trained engineer with a doctorate in artificial intelligence for construction, Morkos is a lecturer at his alma mater. Before founding ALICE, he spent over a decade managing construction projects in more than 20 countries — from rebuilding infrastructure in Afghanistan to automating a $350 million refinery expansion in Abu Dhabi. These experiences revealed to him a core industry flaw: project controls that are rigid, reactive and blind to uncertainty. Under Morkos’s leadership, ALICE (the name is an abbreviation of ArtificiaL Intelligence Construction Engineering) has focused on AI in project planning and scheduling. It’s been used on over $115 billion in global construction to simulate millions of sequencing scenarios, recover from delays, and optimize outcomes. Morkos spoke with Commercial Observer earlier in June about ALICE’s alliance with McKinsey, what it means for both companies and for the construction industry in general, and why he thinks AI is “the bee’s knees.” This interview has been edited for length and clarity. Commercial Observer: ALICE Technologies recently formed an alliance with McKinsey & Company, which usually doesn’t announce such associations. What does this alliance mean, exactly? René Morkos: It means we work together because we have complementary skill sets to push the mission of generative scheduling and construction. We started working with McKinsey more than four years ago. We have slowly gotten closer, and, I think, better, at each other’s respective strengths. I think it’s just been long enough where both parties are comfortable with the other’s skill sets, and, from their sid
bullets: René Morkos, ALICE Technologies CEO, is AI-focused construction optimization platform founder
ALICE deployed on $115B+ global construction; used for sequencing, delay recovery, outcome optimization
McKinsey partnership (April 2024) signals tech-consulting embrace of AI construction planning
stories.summary: René Morkos, founder and CEO of ALICE Technologies (Artificial Intelligence Construction Engineering), has built an AI-driven construction optimization platform deployed across $115B+ in global projects to simulate sequencing scenarios, recover delays, and optimize outcomes. A Stanford-trained AI engineer with 10+ years managing large construction projects globally, Morkos recently formed a partnership with McKinsey & Company to expand ALICE's reach and consulting integration.
stories.impact: ALICE's deployment across $115B+ in construction signals growing adoption of AI-driven scheduling and cost optimization in large projects. For multifamily developers, this suggests rising industry adoption of AI sequencing/scheduling tools that compress timelines and reduce contingency burn—particularly relevant for large, complex builds and mixed-use projects where sequencing risk is high. Early adoption may confer scheduling and cost advantages; expect AI construction tech to become table-stakes for institutional developers managing $50M+ projects within 2–3 years.

stories.title: Mystery bidder on Hawthorne Race Course facility could end planned “racino” development
stories.excerpt: An unknown corporate buyer represented by a shell company is set to purchase the Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney for $90 million, unless another buyer steps in to save the racetrack from likely being closed by the entity.  The suburban racetrack submitted a proposal in bankruptcy court to sell its assets to ALLIMAC 2023 LLC, according to the Chicago Tribune. Previously, Hawthorne president Tim Carey stated that proposals for the site’s redevelopment included data centers, a hot-button issue in city planning across the nation, as well as a $107 million offer. The track is the last remaining professional horse racing […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: ALLIMAC 2023 LLC (shell corp) submitted $90M stalking horse bid for Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney
Bankruptcy sale; mystery bidder; racetrack closed Feb 2025; other bidders until June 30 deadline
Original 2019 racino approval never materialized; data center, alternative uses likely post-sale
stories.summary: Hawthorne Race Course in Stickney, the last professional horse racing facility in the Chicago area, is being sold in Chapter 11 bankruptcy for $90M via a stalking horse bid from ALLIMAC 2023 LLC, a shell company of unknown backing. Hilco Real Estate and Province have contacted ~8,000 buyers; 40 conducted due diligence. The racetrack's heavy industrial zoning and city proximity have attracted multifamily, data center, and alternative-use interest. A planned 2019 'racino' (casino + racing) never materialized due to political opposition and financial woes.
stories.impact: Hawthorne's industrial-zoned, 50+ acre site near Chicago is primed for redevelopment; data centers and multifamily are competing for this large, entitlement-friendly asset. If a data center buyer prevails, a major alternate site is removed from residential pipeline; if a multifamily buyer wins, the site could yield 500+ units depending on zoning upside. Watch the June 30 bidding deadline and final sale price; competitive bids and mystery buyer interest signal strong demand for large Chicago-area industrial land, supporting acquisition strategy in underutilized ring-suburbs with density potential.

stories.title: Historic Queens Bowling Alley Trades for $45M to Multifamily JV
stories.excerpt: The operators of the Whitestone Lanes bowling alley in Flushing, Queens, have gotten the ball rolling on a major multifamily redevelopment. Midtown-based development firm Urban Realty Partners joined Mar Mar Realty, the private development arm of the bowling alley’s multigenerational owners, to acquire the site for $45 million, Commercial Observer has learned. The joint venture will transform the 48-lane bowling alley at 30-05 Whitestone Expressway into dense, multifamily housing. The 81,150-square-foot lot was rezoned several years ago to accommodate more than 406,000 square feet of residential development, much to the ire of the bowling alley’s devoted local patrons. A conceptual plan approved in 2024 detailed a nine-story development with 415 apartments, but the joint venture’s current plans are unclear. The redevelopment is expected to include affordable housing, benefiting from the city’s mandatory inclusionary housing program, the federal Opportunity Zone program and eligibility for the state’s 485-x tax incentive program. The site will also feature public open space along the southern corridor of Farrington Street and 31st Road. Located adjacent to the Whitestone Expressway, the future multifamily building will reside in close proximity to Downtown Flushing, Citi Field and the massive redevelopment at Willets Point. Whitestone Lanes, opened in the 1960s by the Macaluso family, is a longtime local landmark. Once dubbed the “holy grail of bowling” by the New York Times, the business was well known for its pre-COVID 24/7 operating hours and no-frills style. Owner Marco Macaluso first listed the building for sale for $60 million in 2015 and initiated the rezoning process for the site. JLL’s Mike Mazzara, Ethan Stanton and Brendan Maddigan negotiated the recent transaction directly with the joint venture. “Our objective was to identify a buyer with the vision, capitalization and execution capabilities necessary to realize the full potential of the site,” Mazzara s
bullets: Urban Realty Partners + Mar Mar Realty acquire Whitestone Lanes bowling alley (Flushing, Queens) for $45M
81k sq ft lot rezoned for 406k+ sq ft residential; 2024-approved 9-story, 415-unit concept plan
Mandatory inclusionary housing applies; current JV plans unclear; bowling alley closing to redevelop
stories.summary: Urban Realty Partners and Mar Mar Realty (the bowling alley owner's development arm) acquired the Whitestone Lanes bowling alley in Flushing, Queens, for $45M to redevelop the 81,150 sq ft site. The lot was rezoned years ago to allow 406,000+ sq ft of residential development. A 2024-approved concept details a nine-story, 415-unit building; current JV plans are under review. The redevelopment will include affordable units under NYC's mandatory inclusionary housing (MIH) program.
stories.impact: Queens' MIH program is actively driving multifamily density on underutilized commercial sites; Flushing's rezonings and 415-unit approvals signal strong municipal appetite for housing supply. The $45M acquisition for an 81k sq ft lot (~$550/sq ft land) sets a local Queens comp for similar single-use-to-multifamily conversions. Expect pipeline acceleration in Flushing and outer-boroughs TOD-zoned sites; MIH subsidy requirements will compress margins—underwrite at reduced rents and monitor city TIF/PILOT opportunities to improve returns.

stories.title: $580M cultural complex in Woodlawn snags Plan Commission approval
stories.excerpt: Around a half-decade after the Apostolic Church of God unveiled plans for the Woodlawn Central $580 million megadevelopment, key rezoning changes are giving an indication that the project is ready to move forward on the city’s South Side. The initial zoning change application for the 8-acre first phase space was filed in December 2025. The new change could allow the first phase, which includes a 14-story residential building with 104 units, to move forward. 22 of the units created would be affordable. The residential building would also include a 160-key hotel, 23,000 square feet of retail space, 3,500 square feet […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: Woodlawn Central: $580M cultural complex by Apostolic Church; first phase approved by Plan Commission, 104 apartments + hotel + retail
22 affordable units in Phase 1 (14-story residential); full build-out ~1,000 mixed-income/senior units; construction 2027–2028 estimate
South Side Chicago; black entrepreneurs focus; zoning still requires City Council/committee approval
stories.summary: The Apostolic Church of God's Woodlawn Central, a $580M mixed-use cultural complex on Chicago's South Side, won Plan Commission approval for first-phase zoning changes. Phase 1 includes a 14-story residential building with 104 units (22 affordable), a 160-key hotel, 23,000 sq ft retail, and 3,500 sq ft commercial space. Developers project permit lock-in early 2027 and construction completion late 2028. The full build-out envisions ~1,000 mixed-income and senior housing units, a performance venue, and restaurants; zoning still needs City Council approval.
stories.impact: Woodlawn Central's phased, mixed-income approach with black-entrepreneur stewardship signals Chicago's South Side reframing as anchor-driven cultural/residential destination. The 1,000-unit full buildout and affordable mandate suggest CHA/city backing; watch for TIF/PILOT financing that subsidizes affordability. Approval momentum and 2027–2028 timeline indicate South Side supply is accelerating—rent growth moderating in Woodlawn/Bronzeville as affordable density increases. Sponsors planning South Side acquisitions should model lower rent growth vs. West Loop/River North and focus on value-add tenant improvements and operational efficiency rather than price appreciation.

stories.title: B3’s Sydnie + Olivia closes the North Shore’s new construction gap
stories.excerpt: The North Shore of Chicago is entering a new era of growth. B3 has emerged as a leader in the region. The firm specializes in transforming long-dormant assets into the most coveted addresses along the shore. Their newest project, The Sydnie + Olivia, is a groundbreaking 30-unit luxury development that is resurrecting a prime corner of Highland Park, IL after fifteen years of vacancy. We spoke with the team at B3 about the vision behind the project, the demand for high-end townhomes and condos, and their commitment to reviving the region.  Diamond in the rough 812 Laurel Avenue in Highland […]This article originally appeared on The Real Deal. Click here to read the full story.
bullets: B3 Development opens Sydnie + Olivia: 30-unit luxury (18 townhomes + 12 condos) in Highland Park, IL
Site vacant 15 years post-2008 GFC; B3 specializes in dormant-site repositioning; 812 Laurel Avenue corner
Luxury townhome/condo positioning; high-end market focus; North Shore Illinois market leadership
stories.summary: B3 Development's Sydnie + Olivia is a 30-unit luxury residential development (18 townhomes, 12 boutique condos) on Highland Park's 812 Laurel Avenue, a site dormant for ~15 years following the 2008 financial crisis. B3, co-founded by Simon Berger and Adam Jidd, specializes in acquiring and revitalizing long-vacant sites on municipalities' watch lists. The firm moved fast through zoning challenges and community opposition to unlock what Jidd called a 'special piece' of Highland Park.
stories.impact: B3's ability to unlock and revitalize 15-year-vacant infill sites in affluent North Shore markets (Highland Park) signals untapped land value and developer agility. For multifamily operators, this highlights deal-sourcing opportunity in stalled single-use and mixed-use sites in blue-chip suburbs post-zoning changes. However, Sydnie + Olivia is luxury condo/townhome (for-sale), not rental; the demand signal is strong (North Shore affluence, limited supply), but rental multifamily in Highland Park/North Shore may target different buyer segments and could yield higher density if upzoned. Watch for B3's next moves and whether rental conversions or multifamily projects follow this proof-of-concept.
